summary: Gruber criticizes ABC News and Disney for shutting down FiveThirtyEight and immediately pulling its website offline. He condemns the callous manner in which employees learned of their firing through a Wall Street Journal report before being formally told, and the brief, scripted, no-questions meeting that followed. The core argument is that taking a website offline when closing a publication is an act of spiteful disregard for the work it hosted, especially when the parent company is as wealthy as Disney. Gruber contrasts the ephemeral nature of print media with the web's unique ability to preserve and make accessible years of work, arguing that companies have a moral obligation to maintain archives. He notes that hosting a static archive costs virtually nothing for a company of Disney's size.
key_points: FiveThirtyEight staff learned they were being fired from a Wall Street Journal report before being officially told by management in a brief, scripted, no-questions meeting
Disney immediately pulled the FiveThirtyEight website offline after the closure, destroying years of accessible journalism
The web's ability to preserve and instantly access old publications is a breathtaking advantage over print, but companies routinely squander it by pulling the plug on sites
Hosting a static archived website costs practically nothing for a company the size of Disney, making the takedown an act of disrespect rather than business necessity
Disney didn't burn movies from closed subsidiaries like Touchstone Pictures, yet treats web publications as disposable
money_quotes: This guy Karamehmedovic sounds like a real Grade-A dickhead.
It costs practically nothing to run a server for a static/archived website.
It betrays a profound level of disrespect for the work that the site hosted.
Disney didn't burn the movies from now-closed subsidiary studios like Touchstone Pictures.
The ability of anyone, today, to just instantly access week-, month-, year-, and even decades-old stories is a breathtaking advantage of web publications.
But the standard behavior when closing a web publication is to just pull the plug.
When there's a parent company, especially a thriving one, there's no justification for pulling the plug other than spiteful disregard for the work.
From the perspective of a company the size of Disney, it would cost veritable pennies to keep FiveThirtyEight's website around forever. What a disgrace.
key_insight: Pulling the plug on a web publication's archive when closing it is an act of spiteful disregard that squanders the web's unique power to preserve accessible knowledge, especially when it would cost a company like Disney practically nothing to maintain.
